title: TPU v1 Public Structure · How the Synthesis Baseline Drives DFT Implementation and Physical Iteration
oneLine: Synthesis does not hand backend an isolated netlist. It freezes a version-consistent baseline of gate netlist, SDC/UPF, libraries, QoR, LEC, and DFT-ready definitions. DFT then inserts scan/compression/MBIST and begins ATPG, producing a test-enabled version for physical design. Scan reorder, timing, congestion, power, and ECO feedback continue to update the controlled netlist, chains, and patterns.

legend.name: Gate-level netlist
legend.desc: The core stage-⑦ output maps RTL to target standard cells and connectivity. It is the common logical baseline for DFT insertion and physical-design iteration.

legend.name: Constraints, power intent, and models
legend.desc: SDC/UPF, Liberty, scan cells, memory test models, and black-box definitions must match the netlist version. DFT and backend must continue from the same functional intent.

legend.name: QoR / LEC / DFT-ready evidence
legend.desc: Timing, area, and power reports justify release; LEC proves function unchanged; DFT-ready DRC shows clocks, resets, black boxes, and macro interfaces are suitable for the main insertion and early ATPG window.

legend.name: Traceable synthesis baseline package
legend.desc: Tag netlist, constraints, libraries, reports, and version manifest together. Every later DFT insertion, ECO, or rollback can then identify its parent version, changes, and still-valid evidence.

legend.name: Main DFT implementation window
legend.desc: On the synthesis baseline, insert scan, compression, MBIST/OCC, and other structures; start ATPG, fault coverage, connectivity, and functional-equivalence closure; then produce a test-enabled backend version.

legend.name: Physical-design / DFT iteration
legend.desc: Stage ⑧ starts with the DFT-enabled netlist, functional/test SDC, ScanDEF or chain maps, and macro constraints, then feeds scan reorder, congestion, timing, power, and ECO information back to the controlled baseline.

steps.cap: ① Freeze netlist and complete intent together: gate netlist, SDC/UPF, process libraries, scan cells, and memory macro models must be version-consistent.
② Enforce synthesis exit gates: QoR meets targets, LEC is fully equivalent, and DFT-ready DRC has no blocking issue before test insertion.
③ Apply one tag to netlist, constraints, libraries, reports, waivers, and version manifest, creating a reproducible, auditable synthesis baseline.
④ Enter the main DFT window: insert scan/compression/MBIST/OCC and begin ATPG, fault coverage, connectivity, and LEC, preserving evidence for every change.
⑤ Start physical design from the test-enabled version with test SDC and ScanDEF/chain data, then return physical feedback to the controlled baseline.

before: Define the handoff contract: Specify required netlist, SDC/UPF, libraries/models, QoR, LEC, DFT-ready DRC, version manifest, waivers, and naming conventions.
Set release thresholds: Define acceptable WNS/TNS, area/power, unconstrained paths, LEC, and DFT-ready status plus who may approve exceptions before the final build.
Align tool and library versions: Synthesis, DFT, and physical teams must agree on PDK, Liberty, macro models, and scripts so identical names never hide different revisions.
Disclosure boundary: TPU v1's 256×256 array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 4 MiB accumulators, 700 MHz, and 28 nm are public. Its real handoff package and DFT data are not.
during: Export the gate netlist: Ensure hierarchy, black boxes, macro instances, and naming are stable for downstream tools and clear unresolved references, latches, loops, and other blockers.
Synchronize constraints and power intent: Deliver the exact SDC/UPF used for this synthesis run, plus clock, reset, test-port, and macro-test definitions—not a separately reconstructed copy.
Build the evidence package: QoR reports timing/area/power, LEC proves RTL equivalence, and DFT-ready DRC confirms no structural obstacle at the test entry.
Freeze one tag: Record manifest/hash data for netlist, constraints, libraries, report set, script commit, and waivers so anyone can reproduce the baseline.

Bridge DFT and backend through the shared baseline: First produce a version with initial DFT insertion and preliminary coverage for physical startup, then continuously update netlist, chains, test constraints, and patterns under physical feedback.

result.label: Synthesis baseline
result.value: 28 nm gate netlist + matching RTL/LEC reference + manifest/tag

result.label: Constraints and intent
result.value: Functional SDC, UPF, clock/reset/test ports, libraries, and macro models

result.label: Release evidence
result.value: Timing/area/power QoR + LEC + DFT-ready DRC + waivers

result.label: DFT input
result.value: Stable netlist and complete test models for scan/compression/MBIST/OCC insertion

result.label: Backend-start baseline
result.value: Initial DFT netlist + test-mode SDC + ScanDEF/chain map + macro constraints; iterated with physical feedback

after: Re-prove every DFT netlist change: After insertion, test points, or ECO, rerun functional-mode LEC, DFT DRC, ATPG, and coverage. Old evidence does not carry automatically.
Sign back backend reorder and ECO: After chain, clock, or netlist changes, update ScanDEF/chain maps and revalidate final patterns and test-mode STA.
Maintain two-way iteration: Return concrete congestion, timing, test-power, or controllability reports to synthesis/RTL for controlled repair instead of hidden downstream patches.
Archive a reproducible baseline: Retain scripts, logs, library revisions, manifests, reports, and approvals for ECO, re-spin, and post-silicon diagnosis.
Preserve honest claims: This case describes standard large-digital-chip handoff, not the specific tools, scan architecture, or release metrics used by TPU v1.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (public TPU v1 structure, process, and frequency are context only. Versioned synthesis baselines, concentrated post-synthesis DFT insertion and early ATPG, and two-way physical-design iteration are standard EDA/DFT practice, not disclosure of private v1 netlists, scripts, scan architecture, or coverage.)
